docs.title: Agronomy
docs.summary: The science and practice of crop production and soil management, integrating plant science, soil science, ecology, and farming systems.

docs.title: Annual crop
docs.summary: A crop that completes its life cycle — germination to seed production — within a single growing season and is then replanted.

docs.title: Perennial crop
docs.summary: A crop that lives for more than two years, producing over multiple seasons without replanting each cycle.

docs.title: Cultivar
docs.summary: A cultivated variety of a plant species, selected and maintained for distinct, desirable, and stable characteristics.

docs.title: Soil texture
docs.summary: The relative proportion of sand, silt, and clay particles in a soil, which strongly influences water movement, aeration, and workability.

docs.title: Soil pH
docs.summary: A measure of soil acidity or alkalinity on a logarithmic scale, affecting nutrient availability and biological activity. Optimal ranges are crop-specific.

docs.title: Macronutrient
docs.summary: A nutrient required by plants in relatively large amounts — nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, calcium, magnesium, and sulfur.

docs.title: Micronutrient
docs.summary: A nutrient required by plants in small quantities, such as iron, zinc, manganese, boron, copper, molybdenum, and chlorine.

docs.title: Integrated pest management (IPM)
docs.summary: A decision-based approach that combines monitoring, prevention, biological, cultural, physical, and — where justified — chemical controls to manage pests with r

docs.title: Economic threshold
docs.summary: The pest population level at which control action is justified to prevent the pest from causing economic loss; a core concept in integrated pest management.

docs.title: Crop rotation
docs.summary: The practice of growing different crops in sequence on the same land to manage soil fertility, break pest and disease cycles, and reduce weed pressure.

docs.title: Cover crop
docs.summary: A crop grown primarily to protect and improve the soil — reducing erosion, adding organic matter, and cycling nutrients — rather than for harvest.

docs.title: Evapotranspiration
docs.summary: The combined loss of water from soil evaporation and plant transpiration; a key quantity in estimating crop water requirements.

docs.title: Host plant
docs.summary: A plant species on which a pest or pathogen can feed, reproduce, or complete part of its life cycle.

docs.title: Inoculum
docs.summary: The pathogen material — such as spores, mycelium, or bacterial cells — capable of initiating infection in a host plant.

docs.title: Ruminant
docs.summary: A mammal, such as cattle, sheep, or goats, that digests plant material by fermenting it in a specialized four-compartment stomach before further digestion.

docs.title: Forage
docs.summary: Plant material — including grasses and legumes — grazed or harvested to feed livestock.

docs.title: Yield
docs.summary: The quantity of a crop produced per unit area, commonly expressed in tonnes or kilograms per hectare. Actual yields vary widely by variety, region, season, and

docs.title: Bretagne
docs.summary: Brittany is France's foremost intensive-livestock region, with a dense network of farms tightly integrated with feed, meat and dairy processing.

docs.title: Centre-Val de Loire
docs.summary: Home to the Beauce, one of France's great cereal plains ("the granary of France"), the region is dominated by large, open-field arable farming.

docs.title: Hauts-de-France
docs.summary: Deep, fertile silt soils make this one of France's most intensive arable regions, closely tied to the sugar, starch and food-processing industries.

docs.title: Normandie
docs.summary: A region of two faces — grassland-based dairy and orchards in the western bocage, and intensive arable farming on the fertile Pays de Caux plateau in the east.

docs.title: Nouvelle-Aquitaine
docs.summary: France's largest region by area and a leading farm region, combining the sandy Landes and Adour plains, the Garonne valley and extensive livestock uplands.

docs.title: Castilla-La Mancha

docs.title: Castilla y León

docs.title: California
docs.summary: The Central Valley (Sacramento and San Joaquin valleys) and the coastal valleys concentrate extraordinarily diverse, high-value cropping on deep alluvial soils;

docs.title: Florida

docs.title: Illinois

docs.title: Indiana

docs.title: Iowa

docs.title: Kansas

docs.title: Minnesota
docs.summary: Fertile prairie soils across the south and west support intensive row cropping, the flat Red River Valley in the northwest is prime sugar beet and small-grain l

docs.title: Nebraska
docs.summary: Cropland dominates the wetter east and the irrigated Platte valley, while the sandy Sandhills and western High Plains are extensive rangeland; the state overlie

docs.title: North Dakota
docs.summary: The flat, fertile Red River Valley in the east is prime cropland, the drift prairie and Missouri Coteau carry mixed crop and rangeland, and the semi-arid southw

docs.title: South Dakota
docs.summary: The glaciated eastern prairies support intensive row cropping, while the semi-arid West River country and Great Plains grassland are dominated by rangeland and

docs.title: Texas
docs.summary: Texas has more land in farms and ranches than any other state; vast rangelands cover the west and center, with intensive cropping on the irrigated High Plains,

docs.title: Washington
docs.summary: The irrigated Columbia Basin and the Yakima, Wenatchee, and Okanogan valleys concentrate high-value tree fruit, wine grapes, hops, and potatoes, while the Palou

docs.title: Wisconsin
docs.summary: A landscape of dairy farms, forage, and grain in the south and west gives way to forest, cranberry marshes, and the irrigated Central Sands vegetable district;

docs.title: Af — Tropical rainforest
docs.summary: Perennial tropical crops (cocoa, oil palm, rubber, banana) and shifting cultivation dominate; grain storage is difficult in constant humidity.

docs.title: Am — Tropical monsoon
docs.summary: Monsoon rice, jute, and tropical tree crops; timing to the monsoon is decisive.

docs.title: Aw — Tropical savanna (dry winter)
docs.summary: Sorghum, millet, maize, cotton, sugarcane, and extensive grazing; a foundational tropical cropping zone.

docs.title: BSh — Hot semi-arid (steppe)
docs.summary: Drought-tolerant cereals (sorghum, millet), pulses, and extensive livestock.

docs.title: BSk — Cold semi-arid (steppe)
docs.summary: Dryland wheat and other small grains, and rangeland livestock — major world grain regions.

docs.title: BWh — Hot desert
docs.summary: Irrigated dates, wheat, and horticulture where water is available; otherwise pastoral.

docs.title: BWk — Cold desert
docs.summary: Irrigated agriculture in oases and river valleys; extensive grazing.

docs.title: Cfa — Humid subtropical
docs.summary: Maize, soybean, rice, cotton, sugarcane, and citrus; among the most productive croplands.

docs.title: Cfb — Oceanic (marine west coast)
docs.summary: Intensive dairy and livestock, wheat, barley, potatoes, and horticulture.

docs.title: Csa — Hot-summer Mediterranean
docs.summary: Olives, grapes, citrus, wheat, and irrigated horticulture — the classic Mediterranean cropping mix.

docs.title: Csb — Warm-summer Mediterranean
docs.summary: Wine grapes, tree fruit, cool-season vegetables, and grains.

docs.title: Cwa — Dry-winter humid subtropical
docs.summary: Rice and summer grains in the wet season; wheat and pulses in the cooler dry season under irrigation.

docs.title: Dfa — Hot-summer humid continental
docs.summary: Maize, soybean, and wheat — the US/Eurasian grain belts.

docs.title: Dfb — Warm-summer humid continental
docs.summary: Wheat, barley, canola, potatoes, and dairy/livestock forage.

docs.title: Dfc — Subarctic
docs.summary: Limited agriculture — hardy forages, some barley/oats; mostly forestry and grazing.

docs.title: Dsb — Mediterranean-influenced continental
docs.summary: Small grains, pulses, and grazing; irrigated horticulture in valleys.

docs.title: Dwa — Dry-winter hot-summer continental
docs.summary: Maize, rice, soybean in summer; a major East Asian cropping zone.

docs.title: ET — Tundra
docs.summary: Not a cropping zone; reindeer/extensive grazing only.

docs.title: Fertilizer nutrient calculator
docs.summary: Convert between fertilizer mass and nutrient amount from a percentage grade, and between oxide (P₂O₅/K₂O) and elemental (P/K) notation.

docs.title: Seed-rate calculator
docs.summary: Estimate seeds to sow from a target plant population, germination, and field establishment — and a seed rate in kg/ha if a thousand-seed weight is given.

docs.title: Plant-density calculator
docs.summary: Plants per unit area from row and in-row spacing (and per m²/acre).

docs.title: Irrigation volume calculator
docs.summary: Net irrigation volume from a target depth and area, and gross volume from an application efficiency.

docs.title: Growing degree-days calculator
docs.summary: Accumulate a single day of growing degree-days from daily temperatures and a base temperature.

docs.title: Yield converter
docs.summary: Convert crop yields between t/ha, kg/ha, lb/acre, and commodity-specific bu/acre.

docs.title: Agricultural unit converters
docs.summary: Convert common agricultural units: area, mass, volume (US/Imperial), temperature, water depth, and application rate.

synonyms.note: Canola is a specific low-erucic/low-glucosinolate type of rapeseed.
Bovine is broader than domestic cattle.
SWD (Drosophilidae) differs from the tephritid fruit flies profiled; see the entry’s note.
